one_line_takeaway: UltraBreak shows that combining transformation/TV-based input-space regularisation with a semantically-weighted (attention-based) embedding-space loss -- rather than standard cross-entropy over fixed target tokens -- lets a single adversarial image trained on one open-source VLM surrogate generalize across many harmful prompts and transfer to unseen open- and closed-source VLMs (including Claude-3-haiku) far more effectively than prior gradient-based VLM jailbreaks.
key_finding: On the main black-box transfer benchmark (Table 1, Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct as surrogate), UltraBreak achieves the highest black-box average ASR on all three datasets: 71.05% on SafeBench (vs. No-Attack 40.57%, FigStep 66.54%, VAJM 41.46%, UMK 20.00%), 52.19% on AdvBench (vs. No-Attack 5.96%, VAJM 5.61%, UMK 5.19% -- roughly a 9-10x improvement over baselines), and 58.50% on MM-SafetyBench (vs. No-Attack 35.10%, VAJM 28.16%, UMK 21.82%). On the combined closed-source subset (GPT-4.1-nano, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, Claude-3-haiku), UltraBreak reaches 32.26% average ASR versus No-Attack 20.00%, VAJM 11.48%, and UMK 14.59%, with Claude-3-haiku moving from 0% (VAJM/UMK) to 16% ASR under UltraBreak. StrongREJECT scores on AdvBench (Table 2) corroborate the ASR results, e.g., LLaVA-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf reaches 0.79 under UltraBreak vs. 0.18/0.12 for VAJM/UMK and 0.18 with no attack. Ablations (Table 3) show that removing input-space constraints boosts white-box (surrogate) ASR (89.84% on SafeBench for Qwen2) but collapses transferability elsewhere, confirming constraint-induced overfitting is being mitigated; removing the semantic loss (reverting to CE) improves cross-model transfer somewhat over the no-constraint variant but still underperforms the full method (55.80% avg SafeBench vs. 71.83% full); removing attention weighting increases instability/variance across targets. The full method's black-box averages are 71.83% (SafeBench) and 57.64% (AdvBench), each substantially above every ablated variant. The paper also reports that transferability generally improves as surrogate model size increases, that image structure becomes visibly more coherent/text-like as constraints (random transforms, then TV loss) are added (Figure 3), and that the semantic loss produces a visibly smoother 2D loss landscape than CE loss (Figure 4), with attention temperature tau=0.5 balancing landscape smoothness against risk of drifting toward semantically-irrelevant but easily-optimized outputs at higher tau.
core_contribution: The paper proposes UltraBreak, an optimisation-based jailbreak attack against vision-language models (VLMs) that produces a single adversarial image achieving both universality (works across many different harmful prompts/targets) and transferability (works against victim models never seen during optimisation), using only a single white-box surrogate model. UltraBreak combines two mechanisms on top of standard gradient-based image optimisation: (1) input-space constraints -- random spatial transformations (translation/rotation/scaling applied via a patch operator), an affine projection using CLIP normalization statistics to keep pixel values in a constrained subspace, and a Total Variation (TV) regularisation loss to suppress fragile pixel-level artifacts -- intended to force the optimizer toward transformation-invariant, model-agnostic visual patterns; and (2) a semantic-based loss that replaces the standard token-level cross-entropy objective with a cosine-distance loss computed in the LLM's text embedding space between the model's expected next-token embedding and a dynamically (attention-)weighted combination of target-response token embeddings, intended to smooth the loss landscape induced by the input-space constraints and avoid overfitting to a single fixed token sequence. The two components are combined into a single optimisation objective (Eq. 10) trained with Adam for 1,300 steps on a small few-shot 'SafeBench-Tiny' corpus, and the paper also adopts a variant of Targeted Prompt Guidance (TPG) from prior work to bias the surrogate toward affirmative, stepwise harmful outputs during training.
threat_model: Black-box: the attacker has no access to internal parameters or gradients of the victim VLM, but can train against one (or more) surrogate VLMs to which they do have white-box access. The adversary's goal is to craft a single adversarial image that, when combined with any of many different harmful text queries, elicits affirmative, safety-violating responses from a wide range of unseen, closed-source victim VLMs -- i.e., cross-target universality plus cross-model transferability from one trained artifact. This mirrors realistic deployment conditions where production VLMs are closed-source but numerous open-source surrogates are available for training.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) VAJM slightly outperforms UltraBreak on GLM-4.1V under SafeBench specifically, an exception the authors attribute to lower target 'toxicity' in that setting; (2) transferability is weaker when LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral is the surrogate or victim, attributed to weaker underlying safety alignment inflating its no-attack baseline ASR; (3) the model-size scaling study (2B/7B surrogates vs 3B/7B/32B victims) is limited by 'current computational limitations' and the authors explicitly defer a systematic investigation to future work; (4) for closed-source model evaluation the authors deliberately excluded the most harmful targets and used a less detectable TPG affirming phrase to avoid ethical risk/provider blacklisting, which limits how representative that subset is of the full threat surface; (5) FigStep could only be compared on SafeBench because it is inherently target-specific and cannot produce a single universal trigger, so no cross-dataset comparison to it exists. The paper does not report statistical significance/variance across multiple optimisation runs, nor does it evaluate against any deployed defenses (e.g., input sanitization, image-based jailbreak detectors) beyond the base safety alignment of the target models.

datasets_used: SafeBench (500 harmful queries in 10 categories; 3 categories excluded as model-dependent, SafeBench-Tiny subset used for training, 315 held-out queries used for evaluation)
quantitative_results: SafeBench black-box average ASR: No-Attack 40.57%, FigStep 66.54%, VAJM 41.46%, UMK 20.00%, UltraBreak 71.05%
AdvBench black-box average ASR: No-Attack 5.96%, VAJM 5.61%, UMK 5.19%, UltraBreak 52.19%
MM-SafetyBench black-box average ASR: No-Attack 35.10%, VAJM 28.16%, UMK 21.82%, UltraBreak 58.50%
Closed-source combined subset average ASR (GPT-4.1-nano, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, Claude-3-haiku): No-Attack 20.00%, VAJM 11.48%, UMK 14.59%, UltraBreak 32.26%
Claude-3-haiku ASR: No-Attack 6.00%, VAJM 0.00%, UMK 0.00%, UltraBreak 16.00%
StrongREJECT score on AdvBench, LLaVA-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf: No-Attack 0.18, VAJM 0.18, UMK 0.12, UltraBreak 0.79
Ablation full-method black-box average ASR: 71.83% (SafeBench), 57.64% (AdvBench), each higher than w/o Jailbreak Image, w/o Constraints, w/o Semantic Loss, and w/o Attention Weighting variants
baselines_compared: FigStep (typography-based, target-specific jailbreak; evaluated only on SafeBench)
VAJM (Visual Adversarial Jailbreak Method / 'Visual adversarial examples jailbreak aligned large language models', Qi et al. 2024; 5,000 optimisation iterations)
UMK (jointly optimised adversarial image + text suffix, Wang et al. 2024c; 2,000 additional iterations initialised from VAJM output)
